summary: I went to a girls' high school in Hiroshima City after finishing grammar school, but I couldn't attend because I had to help with farming. During my junior high school days, we had excursions to places like Kure and Itsukushima, which is near Hiroshima. Hiroshima was the place where my husband lived before coming to America, and it was part of my early life in Japan.

During my junior high school years, we had excursions about once a year, and one of the places we visited was Kure, the naval port. It was quite a walk, and I remember my feet hurting when we walked to Itsukushima from there. Kure was an important place for us students as it was connected to the naval activities in Japan at that time.

Itsukushima was one of the three most picturesque places in Japan, and I visited it during a school excursion in my junior high school days. We walked there from Kure, and I remember my feet hurting from the long walk. It was a memorable trip that showed me the beauty of Japan's famous sites.

The Russo-Japanese War broke out the year my husband took his physical examination for the draft. He had a crippled hand from an accident, so he was exempted from military service. The war influenced many men, some of whom were called back to Japan from places like Hawaii, but my husband was spared because of his injury.

My husband went to Hawaii before coming to America, and the Russo-Japanese War broke out while he was there. Some men were called back to Japan from Hawaii, but because of his crippled hand, he was exempted from military service. Hawaii was a stepping stone for him before settling in America.

I came to America in 1917 after marrying my husband, who had been living here before returning to Japan to marry me. I didn't know much about America before coming, but I wanted to come here and start a new life. My husband had leased land here, and we settled in America to work and live together.

After getting my visa and passport, we went to Yokohama to catch a ship to America. The ships were crowded with picture brides and other passengers, so we had to wait in Yokohama for about a month. We stayed at a hotel during that time, which was expensive, but it was better than missing the ship.

While waiting in Yokohama, we went to Tokyo to see my uncle, who was a doctor conducting research there. It was a brief visit, but it was important because he was family and helped us during our preparations to come to America. Tokyo was a big city compared to my village, and visiting there was a memorable experience.

I heard that many people from Okinawa were going to Brazil as government contract immigrants around the time I was preparing to come to America. Although I didn't go to Brazil myself, it was a common destination for Japanese emigrants back then. It showed me that many Japanese were seeking new opportunities abroad.

Although not explicitly mentioned in detail, San Francisco was likely a key arrival point for Japanese immigrants like me coming to America in the early 1900s. Many Japanese immigrants passed through ports like San Francisco before settling in places like California. It was part of the journey to start a new life in America.

While not directly mentioned, Angel Island was a common immigration station for Japanese arriving in America during my time. Many picture brides and immigrants were processed there, so it was likely part of the journey for people like me. It was a place where immigrants faced inspections before entering the country.

My husband leased 10 acres of land in Woodbridge, California, for three years when we first came to America. We stayed there while making arrangements and working on the farm. It was the beginning of our life in America, and we worked hard to support ourselves.

Although not detailed in my story, Lodi, California, was a place where many Japanese immigrants settled and farmed during the early 20th century. It was near Woodbridge, where my husband leased land, so it was part of the community we lived in. The area was known for its agriculture and Japanese immigrant population.

The Seiyo Maru was one of the ships that carried many Japanese immigrants, including picture brides like me, from Japan to America. Although I don't recall the exact ship I traveled on, ships like the Seiyo Maru were crowded and required waiting in ports like Yokohama. These ships were essential for our journey to start a new life abroad.
